mechanism: Collect choices from agents in a structured bargaining game with production. Each agent exerts effort that determines payoff allocation. Measure the frequency and context-dependence of which allocation principle (egalitarian, accountability, libertarian) each agent invokes to justify their choices. Classify agents' choice patterns by whether they rationalize higher versus lower payoffs to themselves using different principles. Compare observed choice distributions to the predictions of each pure principle.
domain: behavioral economics, fairness, bargaining

mechanism: Collect responses from subjects describing their understanding of a concept through open-ended descriptions and rating tasks. Apply qualitative and quantitative methods to classify responses into conceptual categories. Compute feature frequencies and correlations among features cited by subjects. Compare resulting feature patterns across multiple empirical datasets to characterize the conceptual structure as a prototype or cluster.
domain: philosophy, conceptual analysis, weakness of will

mechanism: Record eye movement trajectories from readers viewing text sequences. Define regions of interest (target word and adjacent words at fixed positions). Measure a fixation-duration or gaze-contingency metric comparing visual attention to the target when word n+1 is masked versus unmasked. Test for statistical differences in this metric across experimental conditions, using a linear or generalized linear model with fixed effects for word position and frequency.
domain: psycholinguistics, reading, eye movements

mechanism: Assemble repeated cross-sectional survey observations from regions over time. Measure health service utilization indicators (antenatal care, birth delivery, immunization) and concurrent HIV prevalence. Partition regions by HIV burden and time periods. Fit semiparametric models (e.g., spline regression) to health service trends and test for interactions with HIV burden level. Estimate the timing and magnitude of divergence in service utilization between high and low HIV regions.
domain: public health, epidemiology, HIV impact, sub-Saharan Africa

mechanism: Extract panel data of individuals across time periods with relationship status and self-reported well-being scores. Estimate fixed-effects regression models where well-being is the response and relationship formation/disruption events are the predictors, controlling for individual time-invariant confounders. Compute marginal effects (well-being changes) for partnership transitions separately by partnership type and gender. Test for heterogeneous treatment effects by age cohort and gender.
domain: demography, partnership, well-being, Netherlands

mechanism: Present subjects with scenario pairs contrasting action (commission) versus inaction (omission) with matched intentions, motives, and outcomes. Collect moral judgment ratings or binary choice responses from subjects. Tabulate choice frequencies favoring omissions. Decompose judgments into constituent factors (presence of action, salience of alternative causes, actor knowledge) and test their association with omission bias via logistic regression or contingency analysis.
domain: psychology, moral judgment, decision-making

mechanism: Display product packages varying in unit count (e.g., 15 vs. 3 units shown) to subjects. Measure anchoring bias in quantity judgments via self-reported quantity estimates and actual consumption volumes. Analyze judgments and consumption as functions of unit count in linear regression models. Test moderation by visual processing depth or explicit product information. Compute effect sizes comparing high-unit to low-unit conditions.
domain: consumer psychology, anchoring, food consumption

mechanism: Randomly assign schools to two intervention conditions (computer-based with and without adaptive features) or control. Screen first-grade students and identify an at-risk subgroup. Administer reading and spelling assessments to all groups at baseline and endpoint. Compute treatment effect estimates using mixed-effects regression models with random intercepts for schools. Test for differences in effect magnitude between intervention conditions and between at-risk and unscreened samples.
domain: education, early reading intervention, literacy, Norway

mechanism: Present auditory stimuli (tones paired with names) to participants in a classical conditioning paradigm. Record electroencephalographic responses to measure event-related potentials, particularly the P3 component, across trials. Classify trials by stimulus type (own name, different name, unpaired). Compute trial-averaged ERP waveforms and time-frequency decompositions. Test for significant differences in response magnitude and latency across stimulus types using repeated-measures analysis of variance or permutation tests.
domain: cognitive neuroscience, conditioning, event-related potentials

mechanism: Record performances by musical ensembles in live and recorded formats (video, audio, combined). Obtain judged competition outcomes (winners versus non-winners) from domain experts. Show participants videos or audio recordings (modality varied experimentally) and collect binary choice responses selecting the higher-performing ensemble. Compute classification accuracy (proportion correct) across modalities. Test for main effects of stimulus modality using logistic regression and mediation analysis to assess the contribution of visual versus auditory information.
domain: music, group performance, judgment, visual dominance

mechanism: Assemble panel data of countries with gross domestic product growth rates, ethnic diversity measures, and survey responses on government impartiality perception. Define outcome as sustained growth (>= 2% annual GDP per capita for >= 5 consecutive years). Model sustained growth as a function of government impartiality perception and diversity controls via logistic or fixed-effects regression. Estimate country-level correlations between impartiality perception and growth sustainability across 20 nations.
domain: political economy, economic growth, sub-Saharan Africa, ethnicity

mechanism: Measure manufacturing practices (process simplicity, procedural discipline) and performance outcomes (flexibility, responsiveness) at approximately 800 plants in five countries via survey questionnaire. Assemble a data table of plants with practice and outcome variables. Compute correlations between simplicity + discipline index and flexibility metrics. Test the rigid flexibility model (simplicity + discipline jointly predict flexibility) via multiple regression. Compare model fit to trade-off and cumulative models.
domain: manufacturing, operations strategy, rigid flexibility

mechanism: Assign subjects to either a repeated checking condition or a repeated exposure (use) condition with a control stimulus. Measure memory properties (accuracy, confidence, vividness, detail) via questionnaire and task performance at baseline, mid-test, and post-test. Collect state anxiety ratings. Fit linear regression models with repeated measurements as outcome and condition (checking vs. exposure) as predictor. Test for significant declines in memory properties and state anxiety changes across time in experimental versus control groups.
domain: psychology, cognition, compulsive checking, memory

mechanism: Present subjects with epistemological Gettier cases (scenarios where justified true belief does not constitute knowledge) in which the actor performs a morally neutral, positive, or negative action. Collect binary knowledge attribution responses (does the actor know?). Tabulate attribution rates by case type and moral valence. Compare Gettier-case knowledge attribution rates to non-Gettier baseline. Test for main effect of moral valence and Gettier interaction via contingency chi-square or logistic regression.
domain: experimental philosophy, epistemology, Gettier cases

mechanism: Assemble cross-national survey and institutional data with country-level political polarization (measured as dispersion of self-reported political preferences) and public spending (% of GDP). Classify countries as democratic or autocratic. Fit a linear regression model with government size as outcome, polarization as predictor, with interaction by regime type. Estimate correlation coefficients separately by regime type.
domain: political science, political economy, polarization, government spending

mechanism: Assemble survey responses from individuals across five continents with self-reported crime victimization status and political participation measures. Code participation as binary (engaged vs. not). Estimate logistic regression models with participation as outcome and victimization as predictor, controlling for education and demographics. Compute marginal effects and compare effect magnitudes across regions and crime types (violent vs. nonviolent). Test for interactions with satisfaction with democracy.
domain: political science, political participation, victimization, survey

mechanism: Collect survey data on individual values (emancipative vs. traditional) and non-violent protest behavior at two levels: individual and national. Decompose protest variance into within-country and between-country components. Estimate multilevel regression models with protest as outcome and individual values as predictor, with random intercepts for countries and random slopes. Compute the cross-level interaction between individual values and national prevalence of emancipative values to test elevator and amplifier effects.
domain: political science, values, protest, cross-level effects

mechanism: Link survey respondent interview dates to randomly timed news event (police killing in 2014). Partition survey data by respondent race, comparing attitudes assessed before and after the event within each race group. Estimate difference-in-differences effects using logistic regression with outcome as attitude (e.g., trust in police), predictor as event timing, control for demographics. Compute the event effect separately for black, white, and South Asian respondents to test heterogeneity by race.
domain: political science, race relations, policing, diaspora politics

mechanism: Assemble cross-sectional survey data from young adults with substance use variables (cigarette smoking, cannabis use) and psychotic-like experience measures (frequency via CAPE questionnaire; distress from experiences). Fit ordinary least squares regression models with frequency and distress as outcomes separately. Model both outcomes with and without smoking as a predictor in sequence. Test for confounding by comparing cannabis coefficients with and without smoking included. Compute standardized effect sizes (beta values) and p-values.
domain: psychiatry, epidemiology, substance use, psychosis

mechanism: Extract survey responses on immigration concerns and trust in national political institutions across countries and time periods (2002-2009). Assemble country-level covariates (postwar immigration history, far-right party presence). Fit multilevel regression with political trust as outcome and immigration concern as predictor. Include country-level random intercepts and cross-level interactions with immigration history and far-right party presence. Test moderation effects of postwar history and party mobilization.
domain: political science, immigration, political trust, Europe
